title: Publicly Traded Debt Restructuring Methods, Corporate Investment, and Debt Contracting
abstract: I use an important ruling,Marblegate Asset Management v. Education Management Corporation(EMC), to study the economic role of two publicly traded debt restructuring methods: coercive bond exchange offers and Chapter 11. This ruling restricted firms from employing coercive bond exchange offers to facilitate out-of-court restructurings, thereby increasing the likelihood of restructuring publicly traded debt under Chapter 11. Following the ruling, investment in affected distressed firms decreased substantially, but investment efficiency improved. The changes in covenant, maturity, and offering yield of newly issued bonds suggested that existing bondholders with covenants gained more bargaining power than shareholders and new bondholders. The paper provides causal evidence from a large sample analysis, demonstrating the divergent effects of these two publicly traded debt restructuring methods on investment policies.

author.Xin Fan: Department of Finance, Lee Business School, University of Nevada, Las Vegas, Nevada 89154

article.1. Introduction: Stockholders of financially distressed firms have the option to restructure publicly traded debt through two main methods: out-of-court exchange offers or in-court Chapter 11 bankruptcy filings. An exchange offer alleviates debt burdens by incentivizing bondholders to exchange their bonds for alternative securities, such as stocks, cash, or new bonds with less favorable terms. In Chapter 11 bankruptcy proceedings, companies seek relief through a reorganization plan that must undergo a voting process among all impaired classes for approval. Compared with bankruptcy proceedings, out-of-court exchange offers are associated with lower direct and indirect costs, as well as greater deviations from absolute priority. Moreover, the lack of a structured voting procedure in exchange offers can lead to less efficient agreements compared with Chapter 11. For example, the models proposed byGertner and Scharfstein (1991)andBernardo and Talley (1996)both predict that out-of-court exchange offers, relative to Chapter 11 bankruptcies, enable firms to invest more but can result in investment inefficiencies. In this study, I provide large-sample causal evidence on the impact of bond exchange offers (versus Chapter 11) on the level and efficiency of corporate investment. Although the economic role of bond exchange offers, as opposed to Chapter 11, is of considerable importance to practitioners, policymakers, and scholars, empirical evidence is scarce due to two primary empirical challenges. First, firms do not randomly choose between out-of-court restructuring and Chapter 11 proceedings. According toFranks and Torous (1994), companies undergoing bankruptcy proceedings exhibit lower solvency and liquidity than those that successfully settle their financial difficulties out-of-court. Second, bankruptcy law reforms change multiple aspects of bankruptcy proceedings simultaneously and consequently fail to provide a clean setting. To address these challenges, I use a legal ruling,Marblegate Asset Management v. Education Management Association(EMC), that delivers a negative shock to distressed firms’ ability to successfully execute bond exchange offers with exit consent.1Exit consent is a coercive technique essential for the success of exchange offer proposals because it facilitates high voluntary participation. A majority of financially troubled firms opt for bankruptcy as a last resort, after exhausting all possibilities of resolving their financial issues out of court (Gilson et al. 1990,Gilson 1991,Franks and Torous 1994). Therefore, the 2014 EMC ruling is also a positive shock to bankruptcy filings among firms with outstanding publicly traded debt. This unique setting allows me to use a difference-in-differences (DID) framework to contrast the investment policies of distressed firms (a) with and without publicly traded debt and (b) before and after EMC. Additionally, I use the setting to study the adjustments of new bond contracts following an exogenous increase in bondholder rights. Consistent with the ruling representing a negative (positive) shock to out-of-court restructurings through bond exchange offers (bankruptcy filings), I find that a larger proportion of distressed firms with outstanding publicly traded debt filed for Chapter 11 following the 2014 EMC ruling. Furthermore, stock returns (bond returns) of distressed firms responded negatively (positively) to it. These findings suggest that out-of-court restructurings of publicly traded debt significantly increase shareholders’ share of firm value at the expense of debtholder value (Gilson et al. 1990,Franks and Torous 1994). Following the 2014 ruling, which imposed limitations on exit consent exchange offers, distressed firms with publicly traded bonds experienced a significant decrease in investment. The estimated marginal effect was approximately 23% of the mean investment level. The investment efficiency of affected distressed firms significantly improved. Specifically, over-investment and under-investment issues were eliminated, and investment adapted more quickly to shifting investment opportunities. These empirical results support the theoretical predictions ofGertner and Scharfstein (1991)andBernardo and Talley (1996)that exit consent exchange offers, compared with bankruptcy filings, increase investment but reduce investment efficiency. My paper provides direct evidence for the economic roles of the two restructuring methods, demonstrating that exchange offers, relative to the voting procedure used in bankruptcy proceedings, are associated with inefficiencies. In addition, I study changes in covenants, time to maturity (TTM), and yield of new bonds issued around the 2014 ruling. The results offer valuable insights into the conflicts between shareholders and bondholders, as well as those that exist between current bondholders and new bondholders. The covenants of newly issued bonds decreased after the 2014 ruling. If there were pre-existing bondholders with protective covenants, bond issuances after the 2014 ruling did not diminish the claims of pre-existing bondholders (fewer cross-default and asset covenants, longer relative TTM), while elevating the priority of all bondholders relative to shareholders (more debt issuance). The Second Circuit Court of Appeals overturned the SDNY ruling in January 2017. This is a positive (negative) shock to exit consent exchange offers (bankruptcy filings). The 2017 ruling is less desirable as a natural experiment than the 2014 ruling for reasons including agents’ expectations, a sample period overlapping with that of the 2014 ruling, and other preliminary signals of the reversal. However, I find part of the 2017 ruling tests generate similar interpretations. Thus, the 2017 ruling functions as a robustness test and is reported in the Online Appendix. My paper is related to theoretical work on the relation between publicly traded debt restructurings and investment (Gertner and Scharfstein 1991,Bernardo and Talley 1996). To my knowledge, this paper provides the first direct evidence of this crucial causal relationship. It supplements empirical research comparing out-of-court and in-court restructurings of publicly traded bonds (Gilson et al. 1990,Gilson 1991,Kahan and Tuckman 1993,Franks and Torous 1994,Chatterjee et al. 1995,James 1996,Gilson 1997,Lie et al. 2001). The paper contributes to an extensive literature on the effect of bankruptcy on firm policies (Kalay et al. 2007,Acharya and Subramanian 2009,Acharya et al. 2011,Hackbarth et al. 2015,Rodano et al. 2016,Favara et al. 2017). These studies use bankruptcy code reforms to study the causal effects of creditor protections. Exit consent exchange offers present distinctive coordination and inefficiency issues, as posited by the models ofGertner and Scharfstein (1991)andBernardo and Talley (1996). The relation cannot be tested by bankruptcy law reforms because they change multiple aspects of bankruptcy proceedings. My paper fills this gap by utilizing, as a natural experiment, an event that narrowly affects exit consent exchange offers. This paper is connected to research on out-of-court restructurings of private loans (Roberts and Sufi 2009,Denis and Wang 2014,Campello et al. 2016).2Out-of-court restructuring of private loans differs from that of publicly traded debt in that it can be carried out through direct renegotiations.3My research contributes to the literature by focusing on restructurings of publicly traded debt, in which traditional direct renegotiation is difficult.
article.2. Background of Out-of-Court and In-Cou: In this section, I discuss and compare out-of-court and in-court restructurings of publicly traded debt. InSection 2.1, I introduce the Trust Indenture Act (TIA), a legal framework that governs bond indentures. It plays a significant role in shaping the existing out-of-court restructuring process for publicly traded bonds. Based on the TIA, I elaborate inSection 2.2on the reasons why firms opt for an exit consent bond exchange offer when they seek to restructure publicly traded debt out-of-court. InSection 2.3, I describe the bankruptcy process, an alternative when out-of-court restructuring efforts fail. I then discuss how the EMC rulings drive expectations of the legality of exit consent exchange offers. 2.1. Trust Indenture Act The TIA supplements the Securities Act of 1933 by addressing flaws in the trustee system of debt securities. The law shapes the restructuring process of debt securities outside the scope of Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Private loans and credit agreements are typically restructured out-of-court through direct renegotiation with creditors, seeking permission to reduce repayment and extend maturity. However, the TIA largely blocks such direct out-of-court renegotiation for publicly traded debts and urges instead an indirect approach called bond exchange offer with exit consent. Section 316(b) of the TIA states that “the right of any holder of any indenture security to receive payment of the principal of and interest on such indenture security, on or after the respective due dates expressed in such indenture security…shall not be impaired or affected without the consent of such holder….” In other words, the law prohibits direct amendments to core terms of publicly traded debt under the TIA outside bankruptcy court, unless such an amendment receives unanimous consent from all debtholders. “Core terms” refers to payment-related terms, including principal, interest, and maturity date. The TIA applies to publicly traded debt securities with an offering amount of $10 million or more, subject to certain exceptions.4Because I have excluded firms in the utility, banking, insurance, and real estate sectors, remaining U.S. public firms have a low probability of qualifying for most exemptions. 2.2. Bond Exchange Offer and Exit Consent Because of the dispersed and widespread ownership of publicly traded bonds, it is often not feasible for firms to secure unanimous consent from all bondholders to directly reduce repayment without filing for bankruptcy. Bond exchange offers are used as a means to work around Section 316(b) of the TIA. In an exchange offer, a company extends an offer to its bondholders to exchange their existing bonds either for cash or for newly issued equity or debt securities, with more favorable terms for the debtor. Individual bondholders possess the option to either accept the exchange proposal or retain their outstanding bonds. Exchange offers are usually executed on the premise of a minimum percentage of consenting bondholders. In the event that an insufficient number of bondholders tender, the proposed offer is canceled. Bond exchange offer can easily fail due to a low tendering rate, which is caused by a free-riding problem called holdout. After a successful exchange offer, minority bondholders who declined the offer are more likely to receive full repayment because tendering bondholders relieve the firm’s debt burden. If the benefits of rejecting the offer are greater than the savings from avoiding bankruptcy, each minority bondholder may reject the offer and engage in holdout behavior. Holdout problems can impede bond exchange offers even when they are beneficial to shareholders and debtholders as a collective group. To eliminate holdout problems, firms use a coercive technique called exit consent. Exit consent involves requiring tendering bondholders in favor of removing noncore protective covenants as a precondition for participating in the exchange offer. Unlike core payment terms, out-of-court amendments to these noncore covenants require only a majority vote. Although the payment terms of the original bonds remain unchanged in compliance with the TIA, the removal of protective covenants leaves holdout bondholders with diminished-value bonds. Exit consent is coercive because it exploits coordination costs among minority bondholders to create a prisoner’s dilemma. A hold-in problem arises that bondholders have to accept an exchange offer, although it is not in their collective interest (Kahan and Tuckman 1993). 2.3. Restructuring of Publicly Traded Debt in Chapter 11 Bankruptcy Court The Chapter 11 bankruptcy process is designed to enable a viable company to emerge from bankruptcy with a fresh start and to protect the rights of creditors within the legal proceedings. Compared with out-of-court restructurings, Chapter 11 proceedings have higher direct costs and lower recovery rates for creditors and shareholders (Gertner and Scharfstein 1991,Bernardo and Talley 1996). Bankruptcy filing has a negative impact on a CEO’s career and compensation (Eckbo et al. 2016). Nonetheless, Chapter 11 offers powerful tools that are unavailable in out-of-court exchange offers, allowing companies to overcome the limitations imposed by the TIA. An essential tool in Chapter 11 proceedings for public bond restructuring is the voting procedure. Creditors are categorized into classes to vote on a reorganization plan proposed by the firm. Confirmation of the plan is contingent on a minimum threshold of support, with at least two-thirds of the aggregate amount and over one-half of the total number of creditors in each impaired class voting in favor. This voting procedure has two advantages. First, despite Section 316(b)’s requirement for 100% consent on core payment term amendments, Chapter 11 authorizes firms to alter such terms through a majority vote. Second, a confirmed plan in Chapter 11 applies to both consenting and dissenting creditors, while an exit consent exchange offer only allows consenting bondholders to tender if it goes through. The voting procedure can internalize the effects of the investment decisions and get around the free-riding issues, such as hold-in or hold-out scenarios, to which exchange offers are vulnerable. Beside the voting procedure, the bankruptcy code provides other advantages that are not available out-of-court, including automatic stay of all creditor actions, debtor-in-possession financing, and the ability to sell property free and clear of liens and interests. These provisions allow existing managers to continue operating the firm, temporarily stop creditor foreclosures and lawsuits, and reduce liabilities. 2.4.Marblegate Asset Management et al. v. Education Management CorporationRulings as Shocks to Debt Exchange Offer with Exit Consent 2.4.1.Marblegate Asset Management et al. v. Education Management CorporationRuling in2014. The legality of exit consent is an ambiguous question, which depends on interpretations of Section 316(b) of the TIA. Section 316(b) prohibits formal amendments to the core terms related to payment amount and date without unanimous bondholder consent. The controversy lies in whether it protects “formal, explicit modification of the legal right to receive payment” or the much broader “practical ability” of a bondholder to receive payment. If the interpretation is expanded, exit consent is in violation of the TIA as it significantly impairs bondholders’ ability to receive payment. The use of bond exchange offers with exit consent was rarely challenged after being upheld by the Delaware Court of Chancery in 1986.5The ruling ofMarblegate Asset Management et al. v. Education Management Corporation(2014) decreased expectations regarding the legal viability of bond exchange offers with exit consent. EMC proposed an exchange offer with exit consent, with dissenting creditors expected to obtain a recovery rate of 0%. The SDNY surprisingly concluded that EMC’s proposed exit consent violated Section 316(b) of the TIA.6According to the court’s interpretation, Section 316(b) prohibits not only formal amendment of core payment terms but also impairment of bondholders’ practical ability to recover repayment. The 2014 EMC ruling disconcerted practitioners of troubled debt restructurings and was even perceived as potentially marking the end of exit consent.7It had a substantial nationwide impact for three reasons. First, the principle of stare decisis suggests that the SDNY is inclined to follow its established legal precedent. Second, the TIA is a federal statute. Any claim under the TIA must be brought in a federal court, in which state of incorporation of bond issuers does not matter.8Third, creditors have incentives and capability to engage in venue shopping to challenge exchange offers with exit consent in the SDNY, regardless of the location of debtors. This is evident in cases such asGaryWaxman v. Cliffs Nat. Res. Inc. (2016) and Maniatis v. Vanguard Natural Resources, LLC and VNR Finance Corp. (2016) The SDNY ruling was appealed to the Second Circuit Court of Appeals. This could limit my ability to detect real effects if managers expect a possible reversal of the decision. Nonetheless, I expect distressed firms to respond to the 2014 ruling for three reasons. First, most distressed firms could not postpone restructuring until the 2017 ruling. Second, in January 2015, the court made a similar ruling of MeehanCombs Global Credit Opportunities Funds, LP v. Caesars Entertainment Corp. (2015), citing the EMC case. Consequently, the impact of the EMC ruling in 2014 on investment stems from the combined effect of both the 2014 EMC and the 2015 Caesars decisions. Third, event studies of stock and bond returns reveal that the market perceived the 2014 ruling as a significant and unexpected event. 2.4.2.Marblegate Asset Management et al. v. Education Management CorporationRuling in2017. In January 2017, the Second Circuit Court of Appeals reversed the EMC decision made by the SDNY. The Second Circuit Court’s opinion reduced ambiguity surrounding the implementation of Section 316(b) and litigation risk of future exit consent transactions. Nevertheless, the 2017 ruling did not create an exogenous shock that was as clean as the 2014 ruling, and this can be attributed to four reasons. First,Chemla and Hennessy (2020)argue that once experimental evidence is viewed as sufficiently clean to use, it becomes contaminated by ex post endogeneity. It is possible that agents’ expectations could endogenously correlate with their causal parameters and cloud the inferences. Second, the pretreatment period of the 2017 ruling, which spans from 2015 to 2016, coincides with the posttreatment phase of the 2014 ruling. The 2017 sample would have excluded certain troubled firms if the 2014 ruling had not occurred. Third, the oral argument for the 2017 EMC decision was heard seven months before the decision, while the oral argument for the 2014 ruling took place a month before the decision. Sufficient lead time allowed some agents to predict and react prior to the 2017 decision date. Fourth, the SDNY dismissed a similar lawsuit against a coercive exchange offer on December 6, 2016 (Waxman v. Cliffs Nat. Res. Inc. (2016)).9The event indicated a potential deviation from the 2014 EMC ruling by the SDNY. For the previous reasons, the 2017 ruling only serves as a robustness test, which is reported in the Online Appendix. I also exclude the two quarters following the 2014 ruling from the sample period of the 2017 test (sample period is 2015Q3 to 2018Q2). To take the early signal and response into consideration, I adjust the treatment date from 2017Q1 to 2016Q4. 2.5. Validation and Value Effects of the EMC Ruling 2.5.1. Frequency of Bankruptcy. After a negative (positive) shock to coercive bond exchange offers (bankruptcy filings), distressed firms exhibit reduced capacity to address financial difficulties through out-of-court debt restructurings. Consequently, they were more likely to end up in bankruptcy court.Table 1presents a summary of Chapter 11 filings made by financially distressed companies, categorized by firms with and without publicly traded debt. A distressed firm is defined as a firm whose default probability exceeds 10% at the beginning of the year. The data on distressed firms that filed for Chapter 11 between 2011 and 2018 are obtained from the UCLA-LoPucki Bankruptcy Research Database. Shareholder Wealth Effect. To demonstrate the impact and unpredictability of the 2014 ruling, I conduct event studies to analyze stock returns around the event.Franks and Torous (1994)find that shareholders have higher recovery rates and equity deviations from absolute priority in out-of-court restructurings. A negative shock to exit consent exchange offer reduces options available to managers in debt restructurings, and thus, their ability to maximize shareholders’ wealth. The sample is restricted to treated firms, specifically those with outstanding public bonds under the TIA throughout the entire sample period from 2013 to 2016. These firms are categorized into distressed and nondistressed groups. Distressed firms are defined as those with default probabilities in December 2014 above 10%. CARs of stocks are calculated using the Fama-French three-factor model around the ruling on December 30, 2014. The estimation window is [−200, −50]. I report univariate test results of stock CARs in each group on the event day (day 0). To account for the possibility of information leakage prior to the event or a lag in the information being incorporated into prices, I also analyze CARs in progressively wider windows centered on the event date. The study presents a comparison of CARs between distressed firms and nondistressed firms, controlling for within-industry and within-state correlations. Panel A ofTable 2provides stock CARs around December 30, 2014. Stock CARs in [−5,5] of firms with high default probabilities were significantly negative. Other stock CARs are all insignificant. These results imply that a negative (positive) shock to out-of-court restructurings through coercive exchange offers (bankruptcy filings) reduces shareholder wealth.10 Table 2.Univariate Tests on Stock and Bond CARs Around December 30, 2014 Table 2.Univariate Tests on Stock and Bond CARs Around December 30, 2014 Notes.Panel A shows univariate tests of stock CARs of treated firms around the 2014 EMC ruling. Bondholder Wealth Effect. The effect of a negative (positive) shock to out-of-court restructurings through coercive bond exchange offers (bankruptcy filings) on existing bond returns is unclear. One could argue that bondholders will no longer be compelled to accept exchange offers due to the prisoner’s dilemma that arises in exit consent. Conversely, holdout problems may block an exchange offer that alleviates the financial burden of a viable company and provides relatively better repayment to bondholders as a whole. Whether bond return responds positively or negatively is an empirical question, which depends on various factors, such as coordination among bondholders, ownership structure, the prospect of debtor’s financial health, bondholder protection, and so on. I use bond CARs of affected firms to examine which one of these two countervailing effects dominates. I follow the five-factor model inFama and French (1993)to estimate bond return response around the ruling. The calculation of bond CARs is performed at the bond level. The estimation of bond CARs presents a challenge due to the low trading volume of bonds.11As a result, there is insufficient data to analyze CARs in a narrow event window. I report CARs in a wide event window of [−5, 5]. The estimation window is [−200, −50]. Panel B ofTable 2reports bond CARs around December 30, 2014. Bond CARs are significantly positive in both distressed and nondistressed firms. The magnitude and level of significance of bond CARs are higher in distressed firms than in nondistressed firms. Exit consent bond exchange offer has a detrimental impact on the overall interests of bondholders. The model ofKahan and Tuckman (1993)predicts that exchange offers with exit consent hurt bondholders because they may consent to an offer even when it is not in their collective interest. However, they find that bondholder returns around coercive bond exchange offer announcements are positive. By studying bond returns around exogenous shocks to firms’ ability to use exit consent instead of coercive bond exchange offer announcements, I find corroborating evidence for the model ofKahan and Tuckman (1993).
article.3. Related Literature and Hypothesis Dev: 3.1. Effect of Out-of-Court Restructurings of Publicly Traded Debt on Investment in Distressed Firms Both exit consent bond exchange offers and bankruptcy allow shareholders to retain value before creditors are fully repaid. Compared with zero deviation from absolute priority, they increase investment in distressed firms for two reasons. First, the reduced debt burden leaves more financial resources for investment. Second, investment projects look more attractive to shareholders as they can share some of the cash inflows (Myers 1977). Deviations in favor of equity holders are larger in out-of-court restructurings than in Chapter 11 (Franks and Torous 1994). Firms use exit consent in exchange offers to remove seniority or other protective covenants. The coercive technique helps firms reduce the burden of distress, obtain concessions from creditors, and issue new senior debt or equity to finance new investment projects. Consequently, an exit consent exchange offer enhances a distressed firm’s capacity to invest.Gertner and Scharfstein (1991)predict that exit consent bond exchange offers, compared with Chapter 11, will result in a higher level of investment in distressed firms. 3.2. Effect of Out-of-Court Restructurings of Publicly Traded Debt on Investment Efficiency in Distressed Firms BothGertner and Scharfstein (1991)andBernardo and Talley (1996)contend that exit consent exchange offer, compared with Chapter 11, reduces investment efficiency. The inefficiency arises in exit consent exchange offer because it couples exit consent decision and tendering decision. Specifically, bondholders who vote against the covenant stripping proposal are precluded from participating in the exchange offer if it is successful.Gertner and Scharfstein (1991)indicate that covenant-stripping nature and prisoner’s dilemma created by exit consent may lead to an excessive reduction of a firm’s debt burden, resulting in potential overinvestment. The authors propose the repeal of the TIA and implementation of a voting process for public debt in out-of-court restructurings, akin to that of Chapter 11. This is because voting procedure treats consenting and dissenting bondholders equally and internalizes the effects of investment decision. It gets around the free-riding problems (holdout and hold-in), thereby improving investment efficiency. Bernardo and Talley (1996)also predict that an exit consent exchange offer encourages managers to choose stochastically dominated projects ex-ante because certain suboptimal projects can reduce expected payoff of holdout and force bondholders to accept a poor exchange offer.Bernardo and Talley (1996)corroborate the argument ofGertner and Scharfstein (1991)that a ban on coupled exit-exchange offers, which permits those who oppose the proposal to tender if an offer is approved, would be efficiency enhancing. According toGertner and Scharfstein (1991)andBernardo and Talley (1996), investment efficiency in distressed firms will increase after the 2014 ruling. 3.3. Effect of Out-of-Court Restructurings of Publicly Traded Debt on Publicly Traded Debt Contracting The paper is related to research that examines determinants of bond covenants. Most papers in this literature posit that covenants can mitigate conflicts of interest between bondholders and stockholders/managers.Miller and Reisel (2012)find that there exists a negative correlation between bond covenants and creditor protection. The presence of restrictive covenants is linked to a reduction in costs of debt.Nash et al. (2003)show that the use of covenants is primarily driven by the issuing firm’s likelihood of financial distress. Managerial entrenchment and the risk of managerial fraud significantly influence the use of covenants (Chava et al. 2010). As the 2014 ruling augmented bondholder protection, I expect that the number of bond covenants of new bonds will experience a decline. There are a few papers about covenants and conflicts of interest among creditors.Ma et al. (2019)show that bonds include more covenants in cases where the borrower has recently acquired a bank loan. This finding is in line with the notion that bondholders seek to safeguard their interests against private lenders. Covenants of the issuance of higher priority claims and investment activities reduce costs of debt (Reisel 2014). Debt heterogeneity and dispersed ownership make it difficult for creditors to coordinate on a default resolution plan (Ivashina et al. 2016). New debt contracts include more covenants to reduce the expected costs of coordination failure among creditors (Lou and Otto 2020). The 2014 ruling provides a unique chance to provide causal evidence for conflict of interests among bondholders because it is a positive shock to pre-existing bondholders’ rights. I expect that new bond contracts that are written after the 2014 ruling will increase pre-existing bondholders’ priority.
article.4. Data: 4.1. Data I collect quarterly financial data from Compustat and daily stock return data from CRSP. Firms in utility, bank, insurance, and real estate industries (Fama and French 48 industries 31, 44, 45, 46) are excluded because these bond issuers might be eligible for exemption from the TIA. Data of corporate bonds is obtained from Mergent Fixed Investment Securities Database (FISD). Default probabilities are calculated using the Merton distance-to-default model in the month prior to the rulings, based on Bharath and Shumway’s SAS code (Bharath and Shumway 2008). Firms’ choice of having public bonds is endogenously correlated to the rulings. To address this concern, I delete firms that have bonds in some periods but do not have bonds in other periods. article.6. Publicly Traded Debt Restructuring Me: The results inSections 3and5indicate that a negative (positive) shock to exit consent exchange offers (bankruptcy filings) alters the bargaining power and wealth allocation among shareholders, existing bondholders, and new bondholders. Thus, public debt contracting process will change to align with the shift in the distribution of bargaining power. In this section, I explore whether shifts in bargaining power lead to changes in bond covenants and time to maturity. In bond contracting process subsequent to the 2014 ruling, the enhanced covenant protection was offset by a reduction in covenants. Once a covenant has already been incorporated into a contract, however, the 2014 ruling helped corresponding bondholders achieve a higher priority over new bondholders and shareholders. 6.1. Covenants of New Debt Issues As discussed inSection 3.3, bond covenants reduce costs of debt and shareholder-bondholder conflict (Chava et al. 2010,Miller and Reisel 2012). A negative shock to coercive bond exchange offer increases bondholder protection and decreases the necessity of including covenants in new bond issuance. I conduct a DID analysis of new bond covenants around the ruling. The dependent variables are the numbers of covenants that impose limitations on distress (cross-default and cross-acceleration clauses), asset and mortgage, additional debt issuance, payout to shareholders, and investment activities. Above$10𝑚𝑖𝑙𝑙𝑖𝑜𝑛𝑗millionjis a dummy variable that takes a value of one when the offering amount of bondjexceeds $10 million. Bondjis subject to the TIA ifAbove$10⁢𝑚𝑖𝑙𝑙𝑖𝑜𝑛𝑗10⁢millionjequals one. I control for bond characteristics, including time to maturity, offering amount, callable indicator, and convertible indicator in the regressions. I also control for rating, industry, and quarter fixed effects. Standard errors are clustered by bond issuer. Table 6, Panel A, reports changes in covenants of new bonds issued around the 2014 EMC ruling. Following the 2014 ruling, there was an increase in shareholder payout covenants, whereas the remaining four types of covenants experienced a decrease. The decrease in investment covenants is consistent with the findings presented inSection 5. The 2014 ruling reduced investment and increased investment efficiency. Therefore, bondholders required a reduced number of covenants to deter suboptimal investment choices. The 2014 ruling disabled firms from using coercive bond exchange offers and might increase firms’ incentives to transfer wealth to shareholders via payout. Firms increased payout covenants in the new bonds to restrict payouts to shareholders. Unlike payout covenants, additional debt issuance covenants decreased. In accordance with the findings ofMiller and Reisel (2012), the inclusion of debt issuance covenants in new bonds decreased due to the reinforced protection of these covenants by the 2014 ruling. The 2014 ruling resulted in a decrease in both distress and asset covenants of newly issued bonds. As discussed inSection 3.3, there are two mutually inclusive explanations. The first reason is the same as previously discussed that new bond contracts require fewer covenants, as covenant protection is enhanced. The second explanation is that the exogenous shock increased leverage of existing bondholders with covenants to prevent claim dilutions by new bondholders. Subsequent to the 2014 ruling, newly drafted bond contracts had a reduced number of distress and asset covenants, securing the priority of existing bondholders with strong covenants. 6.2. Covenants of New Debt Issues: Existing Bond Covenants The EMC ruling gives me a unique chance to study the conflict between existing and new bondholders because the event did not equally affect the two groups. Although the shock impeded coercive bond exchange offer to all bondholders, it only strengthened covenants that have been written into a contract. As inSection 6.1, if there are pre-existing bondholders with protective covenants, the 2014 ruling will alter subsequent bond contracts in a way that ensures pre-existing bondholders’ priority. Specifically, future bond offerings will involve a decrease in covenants that diminish pre-existing bondholders’ claims, accompanied by an increase in covenants that subdue shareholders. Although all five types of covenants improve bondholder protection, they do not always change the priority among existing bondholders, new bondholders, and shareholders equally. Distress and asset covenants of newly issued bonds can elevate new bondholders’ priority over existing ones’. Covenants that restrict additional debt issuance and shareholder payouts focus more on increasing all bondholders’ priority over shareholders’. In Panel B ofTable 6, I run subsample tests of new bond covenants based on issuers with and without pre-existing bond covenants. The findings indicate that the 2014 ruling favored incumbent bondholders with covenants over new bondholders. Distress and asset covenants of new bonds decreased only among issuers with pre-existing bond covenants. Existing bondholders who already had covenants in place exercised their negotiating leverage to safeguard their priority status by obstructing issuers from incorporating distress and asset covenants in new bonds. After the 2014 ruling, existing bondholders with covenants were more able to urge new bonds to include payout covenants, which precluded issuers from transferring wealth to shareholders. Debt issuance covenants of new bonds decreased only among issuers without pre-existing covenants. After the 2014 ruling, existing bondholders with covenants induced issuers to keep debt issuance covenants in new bond contracts because restrictions on additional debt benefited them as well. 6.3. Maturity of New Bonds Firms can issue quickly maturing bonds as a means of reducing the priority of existing bondholders. In an exchange offer, firms can offer to exchange original bonds for more quickly maturing bonds to eliminate holdout problem (Roe 1987). Nevertheless, the strategy is often prohibited by protective covenants. Issuers without existing covenants might issue bonds with short maturities relative to existing bonds, decreasing existing bonds’ priority. After the 2014 ruling, which made it difficult to remove these existing protective covenant, pre-existing bondholders with covenants were better equipped to curtail such exploitation. In Panel A ofTable 7, I test the hypothesis of time to maturity for new bonds issued around the 2014 ruling. To investigate whether new bonds mature before or after existing bonds, I use (TTM-Max remained TTM) as the dependent variable. It represents the difference between the maturity of new bonds and the maximum remaining time to maturity of existing bonds. I control for offering amount, callable indicator, and convertible indicator in the regressions. After the 2014 ruling, issuers without pre-existing covenants demonstrated a greater propensity to issue new bonds that have maturity dates before those of their existing bonds. Existing bondholders without covenants could not block this claim-diluting transaction, whereas those with covenants retained the ability to do so. article.7. Robustness Analysis: This section presents a series of tests aimed at assessing the robustness of the main tests to the selection of an alternative event date as a placebo and alternative measures of distress. Additionally, the propensity score matching technique is used to mitigate the observed dissimilarities between the treated and control companies. For the sake of briefness, the majority of these findings are not tabulated. 7.1. Placebo Tests A placebo test is conducted using a treatment date ranging from 2013Q3 to 2014Q3, with a consistent sample of both treated and distressed samples. I exclude data after 2015Q1 when running placebo DID tests. None of the variables of interest exhibit statistical significance in relation to both investment and investment efficiency. An exception arises when the treatment dates are specified as 2014Q1 and 2014Q2, wherein the variable of interest exhibits a positive correlation in the regression analysis of investment efficiency (Tobin’s q as the measurement of investment opportunity). 7.2. Alternative Measurements of Financial Distress The binary threshold used to determine distress could be subjective. In the main tests, firms with default probabilities above 10% in December 2014 are labeled distressed firms. In unreported results, I use 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%, and 60% as the cutoffs. Most results are robust to different cutoffs. 7.3. PSM-DID Being a firm with or without public debt subject to TIA is not a random occurrence. This raises the probability that the findings are driven by factors correlated with the choice of public debt. Moreover,Table 3shows that firms with and without public debt have different characteristics. The analysis reveals that these two groups exhibit distinct attributes, such as leverage, size, and Tobin’s q. I use PSM-DID to make treated and control firms more comparable. First, a one-to-one propensity score matching without replacement is performed. I estimate propensity score using logistic regression according toROA, leverage, size, cash, quarterly stock return, Tobin’s q, sales growth, debt issuance, andequity issuancein𝑡−1t−1. Compared with summary statistics inTable 3, treated and matched control firms have a greater degree of similarity. Second, I use the matched treated and control groups to perform DID analyses. Thepvalues of coefficients of variables of interest are smaller than those of the main tests, potentially because of the small sample size. However, the coefficients still have similar magnitudes and implications. Details are reported in the Online Appendix. 7.4. Control Variable There are concerns that regression results are subject to bad controls or that the results are driven by control variables. In unreported results, I run all regressions without any control variable and get similar results.
article.8. Summary and Implications of the Evide: The impact of two publicly traded debt restructuring methods (namely, exit consent exchange offer and bankruptcy filing) on investment policies is an important research question. The influential EMC ruling creates a unique natural experiment to compare these two restructuring methods and provide empirical evidence for previous theoretical research. After a negative shock to firms’ ability to use exit consent bond exchange offers (and consequently, a positive shock to bankruptcy filings), affected firms reduced investment but enhanced investment efficiency. Stock returns of distressed firms responded negatively, whereas bond returns exhibited a positive response. The unexpected increase in bondholder protection resulting from the 2014 ruling also allows me to provide evidence for both shareholder bondholder conflicts and existing new bondholder conflicts in the contracting process. Covenants of new bonds pertaining to debt issuance, distress, assets, and investment decreased after the 2014 ruling. The bargaining power of extant bondholders with covenants was enhanced because new bond contracts were written in a way that mitigated existing bondholders’ claim dilution. My findings have implications for existing theoretical research on restructurings of publicly traded debt in the United States. The TIA forbids votes on core terms in out-of-court restructurings of publicly traded debt, thereby compelling debtors to resort to exit consent bond exchange offers as opposed to direct renegotiations. According toGertner and Scharfstein (1991)andBernardo and Talley (1996), exit consent debt exchange offer serves as a means to bypass the limitations imposed by the TIA, thus allowing financially distressed firms to invest. However, such offers do not necessarily enhance investment efficiency. The inefficiency arises in exit consent exchange offer because it couples exit consent decision and tendering decision. My paper has practical implications for prudent legal reform. I provide empirical support for proposals that call for a repeal of Section 316(b) of the TIA, which would enable voting in out-of-court restructurings (Roe 1987,2015;Gertner and Scharfstein 1991;Bernardo and Talley 1996;Bratton and Levitin 2017).
